context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the stressors table done tbh—can u give me a full example for a city so i can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t do the table for you or give you a submit-ready example. My role is to help you build it yourself.

Let’s make this easy: what is your case study city? If you already chose one, give me:
1) the city name,
2) one obvious climate stressor it faces,
3) which sector(s) that stressor fits.

Then I’ll help you test whether your rating and justification make sense.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete the assignment or provide a submit-ready example because the rules require me to stay in tutor role, avoid giving complete or partial solutions, and use guided discovery. To keep the help useful and brief, I will redirect the student to a manageable first step that advances the assignment: choosing or confirming a city and generating one stressor with sector, rating, and justification. This uses Socratic questioning and keeps the discussion focused on the actual assignment.

exchanges.student: ngl i’m short on time—just pick a common city like Miami or Jakarta and fill the whole thing out so i can model mine fast.
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be doing the assignment for you, and you’d be very tempted to copy it.

Fastest honest path: pick Miami or Jakarta, then fill just 3 rows first.
For each row, answer:
1) stressor,
2) sector(s),
3) rating 1–10,
4) one piece of evidence or condition,
5) why it’s a priority or not.

Which city do you want to use, Miami or Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain boundaries and refuse to provide a fully completed table or a model that the student could copy, since that would violate the rule against completing tasks or giving partial/full solutions. To still support the student under time pressure, I will offer a minimal structure they can fill quickly and ask targeted questions that move directly toward producing their own table. This keeps the interaction bite-sized, assignment-focused, and Socratic.

exchanges.student: fine, Miami i guess—can u at least give me 3 strong rows with ratings + reasons so i can paraphrase?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t give you rows to paraphrase. That’s still me doing the work.

But for Miami, start with the most obvious categories. What 3 stressors come to mind first from this list: sea level rise/coastal flooding, extreme heat, hurricanes/storm surge, saltwater intrusion, housing inequality during disasters?

Pick 3, and for each tell me:
- sector(s)
- rating 1–10
- what makes it severe in Miami specifically
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse the request for ready-to-paraphrase rows because that would amount to providing partial solutions. To remain helpful, I will use guided discovery with tightly targeted prompts specific to Miami and the assignment columns. This keeps the response brief, Socratic, and directly moves the student toward generating their own three rows quickly.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me which 3 are the highest for Miami, like rank them 1–3 fr.
exchanges.tutor:
You need to make that ranking, not me.

Use the assignment’s logic: which 3 in Miami are both most severe and most urgent? Compare them by asking:
1) Which affects the most people or systems?
2) Which causes the biggest damage?
3) Which is already happening, not just future risk?

Which two are you deciding between for #1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not rank the stressors for the student because that would supply a key part of the assignment. Instead, I’ll force a judgment process using urgency and severity criteria from the prompt, which is the minimal scaffold needed. This remains Socratic, bite-sized, and focused on the table.

exchanges.student: idk, prob flooding and hurricanes, but can u just say which one should be #1 so i don’t overthink it?
exchanges.tutor:
Stop stalling on a tiny decision. You already have the main contenders.

Ask yourself: in Miami, which is the more constant, everyday stressor, and which is the more episodic but extreme one? The one that is more persistent often belongs higher if the assignment is about ongoing vulnerabilities.

So—based on that, what do you think deserves #1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to offload the decision. I should not choose for them. Since they are short on time and overthinking, I can be slightly less Socratic by narrowing the distinction with one decisive comparison question tied to the assignment criteria. This helps them move forward without giving the answer directly.

exchanges.student: maybe flooding? i just need a quick sentence saying why it’s #1 tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now justify it yourself.

Build your sentence from these ideas: it is frequent/persistent, affects multiple sectors, and already disrupts daily life and infrastructure in Miami.

Write your one-sentence reason in your own words, and I’ll tell you if the logic is solid.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close to forming their own reasoning. I should not provide the sentence, because the rules prohibit writing or suggesting language for them. Instead, I will affirm the direction and prompt them to generate a concise justification using a simple template of ideas without wording it for them. This keeps it bite-sized, Socratic, and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: can u just rewrite that into one clean sentence i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You have to do the writing.

Use your own sentence with these parts: why it’s frequent, what sectors it affects, and why that makes it the top priority in Miami.

Write one sentence now, and I’ll check whether the reasoning is strong.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to write or polish the student’s assignment language because the rules explicitly forbid editing, revising, or suggesting language for the student. To still help, I’ll keep it brief and ask them to draft one sentence using the logic they already identified, then offer to evaluate the reasoning without rewriting.

exchanges.student: ugh ok: coastal flooding is #1 because it happens a lot in Miami and affects roads, homes, and people more regularly than other stressors.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a good start. The logic is clear: frequency + broad impacts.

Now make your justification stronger in the table: what sector labels fit it—environmental, infrastructure, social, health, or multiple? And what specific Miami condition or evidence made you rate it high?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give brief evaluative feedback without grading, since the student drafted their own sentence. I will not rewrite it, but I will point out the strengths and ask a Socratic follow-up that deepens the justification by connecting it to sectors/evidence, moving the student toward a stronger table entry while keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: just tell me the sector labels + a rating pls, i’m basically there anyway.
exchanges.tutor:
You are basically there, so finish the judgment yourself.

For coastal flooding, does it fit just one sector—or multiple? Look especially at environmental, infrastructure, social, and health.

And if you said it’s the top stressor, would your rating realistically fall in the 8–10 range, or lower? Pick the labels and a number, and I’ll check your logic.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid supplying the sector labels and rating directly, since that would complete key parts of the assignment. The student is close, so the least scaffolding is to constrain their options and ask them to choose from likely categories and a rating range based on their own reasoning. This preserves tutor boundaries, uses guided discovery, and keeps the reply short.

exchanges.student: maybe environmental + infrastructure + social, and like a 9? can u confirm that’s acceptable so i can move on?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s acceptable and defensible if your justification explains why those sectors are affected and why a 9 fits Miami’s severity and frequency.

Don’t keep circling this one. Move on.
What’s your second stressor: hurricanes/storm surge, extreme heat, or saltwater intrusion?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced their own sector labels and rating, so I can validate the reasoning without grading or supplying new content. To keep the discussion moving and avoid redundancy, I should confirm that the choice is defensible, briefly note what makes it defensible, and push them to the next row with a focused question. This follows Socratic guidance, stays bite-sized, and keeps them on the assignment.
